entry.title: Enhancing SAP Analytics Cloud Dashboards with Image-Based Filters
entry.content:
In today's data-driven world, visual appeal and interactivity play a crucial role in how users engage with dashboards. In SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC), we often use filters to segment data based on different criteria. In this blog, I’ll share an approach to implementing image-based filters in the dashboard. Instead of a traditional checkbox for filtering, I utilized images as clickable filters, enhancing user experience while making the data selection process more intuitive.

Implementation Overview

I created a simple dashboard with continent data. To create an interactive image-based filter, I set up five script variables, each defined as an integer and initialized with a default value of 1. These script variables are  linked to images representing different continents, enabling effective tracking of user interactions.

CSS

To provide users with visual feedback when they interact with the images, I created two CSS classes.

 

.highlight-image {
    border: 2px solid #000000;
    background-color: #eaf0f0; /* Highlight color */
    opacity: 0.7; /* Slightly transparent to indicate selection */
}

.normal {
    /* Normal state styling */
}

 

When an image is clicked, it receives the highlight-image class, making it visually distinct. Clicking the image again reverts it to the normal class.

Script

For the first continent image, I implemented the following JavaScript code in the on-click edit script.

 

ScriptVariable_1 = ScriptVariable_1 + 1; // Increment the script variable

var Script_Array = [ScriptVariable_1, ScriptVariable_2, ScriptVariable_3, ScriptVariable_4, ScriptVariable_5];
var CC_Array = ["Africa", "Asia", "Europe", "North America", "South America"]; // Array of continents
var emptyArray = ArrayUtils.create(Type.string); // Create an empty array for filtering
var j = 0; // Index for the empty array

// Loop through script variables to build the filter array
for (var i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
    if (Script_Array[i] % 2 === 0) { // Check if the script variable is even
        emptyArray[j] = CC_Array[i]; // Add the corresponding continent to the empty array
        j++;
    }
}

// Apply filters based on selected images
if (emptyArray.length === 0) {
    // If no images are selected, apply filter for all continents
    Population.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Continent", CC_Array);
    GDP_Per_Capita.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Continent", CC_Array);
    Inflation_Rate.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Continent", CC_Array);
    Tax_Revenue.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Continent", CC_Array);
    Export_Import_Value.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Continent", CC_Array);
    Interest_Rate.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Continent", CC_Array);
    Continent_Details.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Continent", CC_Array);
} else {
    // If images are selected, apply the corresponding filters
    Population.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Continent", emptyArray);
    GDP_Per_Capita.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Continent", emptyArray);
    Inflation_Rate.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Continent", emptyArray);
    Tax_Revenue.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Continent", emptyArray);
    Export_Import_Value.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Continent", emptyArray);
    Interest_Rate.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Continent", emptyArray);
    Continent_Details.getDataSource().setDimensionFilter("Continent", emptyArray);
}

// Toggle CSS class for image highlighting based on script variable state
if (ScriptVariable_1 % 2 === 0) {
    Image_1.setCssClass('highlight-image'); // Apply highlight class if even
} else {
    Image_1.setCssClass('normal'); // Revert to normal class if odd
}

console.log(emptyArray); // Log the current empty array for debugging

 

Refresh Functionality

The dashboard also includes a refresh image that resets all filters and returns the dashboard to its initial state. When clicked, the following code executes.

 

// This code is executed when the refresh image is clicked

// Remove dimension filters from all data sources related to the "Continent"
Population.getDataSource().removeDimensionFilter("Continent"); // Removes filter from Population chart
GDP_Per_Capita.getDataSource().removeDimensionFilter("Continent"); // Removes filter from GDP Per Capita chart
Inflation_Rate.getDataSource().removeDimensionFilter("Continent"); // Removes filter from Inflation Rate chart
Tax_Revenue.getDataSource().removeDimensionFilter("Continent"); // Removes filter from Tax Revenue chart
Export_Import_Value.getDataSource().removeDimensionFilter("Continent"); // Removes filter from Export/Import Value chart
Interest_Rate.getDataSource().removeDimensionFilter("Continent"); // Removes filter from Interest Rate chart
Continent_Details.getDataSource().removeDimensionFilter("Continent"); // Removes filter from Continent Details chart

// Reset the CSS class of all images to 'normal'
// This visually indicates that no images are currently selected
Image_1.setCssClass('normal'); // Reset Image 1
Image_2.setCssClass('normal'); // Reset Image 2
Image_3.setCssClass('normal'); // Reset Image 3
Image_4.setCssClass('normal'); // Reset Image 4
Image_5.setCssClass('normal'); // Reset Image 5

 

This code removes all dimension filters related to the continent from each chart and table in the dashboard, ensuring that all data is displayed. Additionally, it resets the CSS class of all images to normal, visually indicating that no images are selected.

How It Works

  1. Incrementing the Script Variable: Each time an image is clicked, its associated script variable increments by 1. This allows us to track whether the image is selected (even) or unselected (odd).

  2. Building the Filter Array: The script uses a for loop to iterate through all script variables. If a variable is even, the corresponding continent from CC_Array is added to the emptyArray.

  3. Applying Filters:

    • If the emptyArray is populated (indicating that at least one image is selected), the dashboard applies filters based on the selected continents.
    • If no images are selected (the emptyArray is empty), the filter is set to include all continents from CC_Array.

  4. Visual Feedback: The script toggles the CSS class for each clicked image based on the state of its associated script variable, providing immediate visual feedback to the user.

  5. Reusability: This logic is repeated for all images with the respective other script variables, ensuring a consistent user experience across the dashboard.

  6. Resetting Filters: The refresh button removes all applied filters, resetting the dashboard to its original state and displaying all data, while also reverting all images to their default visual style.

Dashboard Output

The dashboard, built with sample continent data, displays images representing each continent.

Initially, I clicked on Africa, Asia, Europe, and South America, which resulted in those images being highlighted and filters applied solely to the selected continents.

When I re-clicked any of the highlighted images, they returned to their normal state, and the corresponding filters were removed.

Also, clicking the refresh image in the dashboard resets all filters, restoring the dashboard to its initial state with all data visible.


Conclusion

This blog covered the implementation of an image-based filtering system in SAP Analytics Cloud, allowing users to visually filter with images. This approach enhances user interaction and makes data exploration more intuitive, offering a valuable technique for improving dashboards.

Hope this inspires you to enhance your own dashboards!

Note

Please be aware that using this method may lead to performance issues when applied to dashboards with a large number of KPIs. It is recommended to implement this approach for smaller dashboards with fewer KPIs to ensure optimal performance.

entry.title: The Sun Isn't Falling The Horizon Is Moving Up
entry.content:
The Beatles' line about "the eyes in her head see the world spinning round" in that sunrise and sunset are as much from your earthly perspective as your cosmic one applies to the coming and going of discussion forums.

As I started this post I saw I appear to have 2 other posts in draft state in this Welcome Corner space, except they also show as published and are quite old (one platform removed). And a recent draft I completed to the point of sending for approval when simultaneously that space was being frozen from new content. Augh, as Charley Brown always said when hit with a block unexpectedly yet also repeatedly.

Published yet drafts

My Spock logic take is these were degraded in state during the last platform switch, as I am pretty sure my  central blog control had no old drafts stuck. Occasionally I'll see this in other topic areas, including phantom comments I don't recall starting, and having no odd cat-paw-driven random keystrokes. If I get ambitious I open the comment and then back it out, as trivial ghosts.

The closest SAP managed tag I could find for the creation/modification/freeze/off-lining of content on this SAP Community site was NetWeaver ABAP Data Archiving. It's related in general even if no R/3 system is directly behind community.sap.com. Individual posts, from a forbidden phrase comment to a marketing plant to a personal privacy removal mass request, can come and go via a published, shared, kind-of-well known methodology. Spaces and/or topic areas differ as they are deliberate group changes.

Social Sign-On Stuff

During the move to the current platform, certain content was to have been skipped over, as one might purge records past their retention periods. It got complicated from what I observed, and not as much went away as might have done. The recent freeze weekend for introducing another SAP product line exemplifies the usual acquire-assimilate-absorb process software vendors do, besides shipping code.

Forums Closed for Maintenance

The walled-domain responsibility assignments that have evolved for the SAP Community may be considered to cover a spectrum of time and interest on the part of topic space owners/curators. Maybe lots of questions come up and it's easier to point to a post than to tackle individual threads with repetitive answers. Maybe the team gets reorganized, and those who were active now aren't.   

People Dot SAP Dot Com Cannot Be Reached

How does it work? How should it work?

What I saw in the Information Architecture space was a "thank you, we're turning out the lights." I asked on back channels, as diplomatically as I could given the potential root causes, and didn't get clarity. Other than I could ask to have my draft moved to another space. I waited to see if anyone noticed the alert that was to have been raised when I asked to publish. Quiet since.

Announcing an "end-of-life" passage for community web content has to have specific process steps for maximum community awareness and recognition. If an SAP product line is shelved, no new features or releases for years, the content could be frozen then if necessary erased. It's happened to a few sites along the way, SAP Jam, the SAP community wiki (transformed into another appearance), and will keep happening as interest from writers and readers waxes and wanes.

I'll leave this 3 or 4 word per line non-haiku as my exit music.

 

#
 sunset post of SCN groups:
 birth, life, death, repeat
 code, ship, bugfix, repeat
 tune in, turn on, drop out
 post, comment, flail, quit

 

The title comes from the Firesign Theatre: “…but the sun is going down!” “Oh, no, no! You are confused! The horizon is moving up!” -- https://vinyldistractions.com/firesign-theatre-how-can-you-be-in-two-places-at-once-when-youre-not-anywhere-at-all/

entry.title: 1 Jahr SAP Change Basecamp: Der Change Management Kompass durch den (SAP) Transformations-Dschungel
entry.content:
Befindest du dich gerade selbst im SAP Projekt Dschungel und möchtest den damit einhergehenden Wandel sinnhaft und nachhaltig vorantreiben?
Dann könnte das SAP Change Basecamp genau das richtige für dich sein, um dir praktische Orientierungshilfen für deinen Projektalltag als Change Manager:in anzueignen.

Wie eignet man sich die notwendigen Kenntnisse und Fähigkeiten beim Thema OCM an? 

Etappe 1: Eine Reise beginnt mit einer Vision

Da es nicht nur dir, sondern vielen anderen ebenso geht, dass der Weg durch den SAP Dschungel Orientierung bedarf und häufig rein technisch betrachtet wird, ist die Idee für das SAP Change Basecamp  entstanden. Visionäre Transformationsprojekte und eine veränderte IT-Landschaft bieten viele Chancen ein Unternehmen weiterzuentwickeln und zu digitalisieren. Doch ein IT-System ist nur so gut, wie die effektive Nutzung der Anwendenden es zulässt. Daher sind die Ansatzpunkte für ein effektives Change Management entscheidend, um eine solche Vision Realität werden zu lassen.

Organizational Change Management (kurz: OCM) ist dementsprechend ein essenzieller Bestandteil, um Mitarbeitende einzubinden und eine gewinnbringende Nutzung des neuen Systems zu erreichen. Dabei ist nebensächlich in welcher Transformation du gerade steckst, ob in SAP S/4HANA Projekten oder in einer Implementierung von weiteren SAP Lösungen wie die Einkaufssoftware Ariba oder das Personalmanagementsystem SuccessFactors.

Durch das frühzeitige Einplanen von OCM kann sichergestellt werden, dass nicht nur der Weg eine im positiven Sinne aufregende Reise wird, sondern auch, dass auftretende Turbulenzen kontrolliert überstanden werden und Betroffene zu Beteiligten werden.

Wie ist diese Reise gestartet?

Etappe 2: Eine gut durchdachte Reiseroute als Grundlage für das Basecamp

Bevor das erste SAP Change Basecamp gestartet ist, wurde es im Digital Change & Adoption Team in der SAP Beratung erprobt und dabei mit dem Repertoire aus unterschiedlichen Projekteerfahrungen und Industriewissen angereichert. Nach der internen Ausgestaltung wurde das Format 2023 extern pilotiert, sodass weiteres Feedback eingearbeitet werden konnte, um zukünftigen Teilnehmenden eine bestmögliche Erfahrung bieten zu können.

Welche Erfahrungen konnten gemeinsam gemacht werden?

Etappe 3: Das SAP Change Basecamp als Kompass durch den Projekt Dschungel.

Um konkrete Fragestellungen aus dem Projektalltag aufzugreifen und die Möglichkeit zu bieten auch ohne Vorkenntnisse Fähigkeiten im Change Management in SAP Projekten aufzubauen und diese zu erproben, bietet das SAP Change Basecamp genau diesen benötigten Erfahrungsraum.

Innerhalb von zwei Tagen lernt man die wichtigsten Stationen eines Change-Prozesses nach der SAP ACTIVATE Methodologie über Impulse von Change Management Berater:innen kennen und wendet diese in praxisnahen Übungen an.

Das Format umfasst unter anderem Themen wie Leadership Alignment, Stakeholder Management, Change Impact Analyse und Change Effektivitätsmessung. In Kleingruppen werden kurze Exkursionen zu den jeweiligen Themen unternommen und mit jedem Stopp wird etwas Neues über die Methodik erlernt. Begleitet von einer Fallstudie können alle Inhalte anhand eines fiktiven SAP S/4HANA Kundenprojektes angewandt, reflektiert und diskutiert werden.

Zusätzliche gibt es Einblicke, wie Transformationsprojekte mit OCM allgemein entlang der SAP ACTIVATE Methodologie begleitet werden können. Dabei ist zu beachten, dass kein Transformationsprojekt exakt wie ein anderes abläuft und es immer individuelle Betrachtungen geben sollte, um einen größtmöglichen Mehrwert für das jeweilige Projekt und somit Unternehmen zu erreichen. 

Mit diesem Vorgehen sind die Teilnehmenden gerüstet, um das Erlernte direkt im eigenen Unternehmensalltag einzusetzen.

Was macht das SAP Change Basecamp besonders?

 

Etappe 4: Erinnerungswürdige Reisen werden von anregenden Mitreisenden begleitet

Um den Hands-on Charakter des Formates zu leben, findet das Basecamp im Schulungszentrum der SAP in Walldorf statt. So können die Teilnehmenden ihren Arbeitsalltag verlassen und Personen aus unterschiedlichsten Unternehmen und Branchen kennenlernen. Die Einblicke in die verschiedenen Zeitpunkte und Set-up von Transformationsprojekten, Erfahrungen mit Herausforderungen und Widerständen kommen zur Sprache und bereichern den Workshop jedes Mal auf ein Neues. Alle Teilnehmenden sind somit gemeinsam auf Erkundungstour und das Engagement sowie das Interesse für das Thema OCM spürbar.

Bei dem Basecamp steht das gemeinsame Lernen über Change Management und der Spaß am Entdecken im Mittelpunkt.

Ein Highlight für die Moderator:innen entlang der Reiseroute sind die positiven Rückmeldungen der Teilnehmenden, die nun für personenbezogenen Situationen und Wetterlagen im SAP Projekt besser gewappnet sind.

Wir haben bereits mehrfach die Möglichkeit gehabt das SAP Change Basecamp durchzuführen und Teilnehmenden zu SAP Cloud Change Management Champions (Link zum Badge) auszubilden.

Bisher sind mit uns bereits Personen aus dem Bereich Personalmanagement gereist, die ihren Arbeitsalltag noch holistischer angehen wollten. Weiterhin haben uns Change Berater:innen begleitet, die ihre Fähigkeiten spezifisch für SAP Projekte weiter ausbauen wollten. Auch Projektmanager:innen, die SAP-Transformationen leiten und ihre Fähigkeiten im Bereich OCM ausbauen möchten, waren dabei.

Wann geht die nächste Reise los? 

 

Etappe 5: Nach der Reise ist vor der Reise!

Die nächsten Reisen für das SAP Change Basecamp stehen bereits fest und wir gehen auf Erkundung am:  

 

9. – 10. Oktober 2024

und

27. – 28. November 2024.

 

Du hast Interesse Teil der nächsten Reisegruppe zu sein?
Dann besuch gerne die Anmeldewebsite zum SAP Change Basecamp.
Wir freuen uns auf dich!

Neben den bestehenden Terminen und den stetigen Weiterentwicklungen wird zukünftig auch die Möglichkeit bestehen eine feste Reisegruppe anzumelden, um kundenspezifische Trainings anzubieten. Ab sechs Personen ist dies daher auch vor Ort beim Kunden möglich.

Und auch das SAP Change Basecamp selbst wird im kommenden Jahr neue Orte entdecken, da das Format neben dem Schulungszentrum in Walldorf perspektivisch an weiteren Orten stattfinden wird.

Unabhängig von den neuen Entwicklungen und Orten, die wir erkunden, bleibt unser Ziel stets dasselbe: eine inspirierende und aufregende Reise anbieten, um das Wissen und die Fähigkeiten aller Teilnehmenden zum Thema Change Management zu erweitern.

Und jetzt: Auf zur nächsten Reise… 🙂

entry.title: Error message no. AIST009: Profit Center change in Investment order and Asset Under Construction.
entry.content:
In this blog I would like to explain how to change Profit Center in Investment order and Asset Under Construction. Error message no. AIST009:
Please note: In my case Profit center was not assigned to any of the cost center master data. Profit center was deriving from custom table based on Cost center But same case might useful when you change PC assignment in Cost center master data. This part I have not tested.


Here is an example of Investment Order and AUC master data.

When we try to change profit center in Asset master data in AS02:


When we try to change profit center in Investment Order master data in KO02:

Usually, Asset Under Construction is created with reference to Investment Order. As both have linked each other we cannot change profit centre individually at AUC or IO level.


Here the solution:
Step 1: Go to KO02 transaction, Change the profit center, then click on Extra and select Asset Under Construction as below.

Step 2: Then it will open AUC with edit mode. Here you change the profit center and press enter. You will get a pop up. Select the option as per your requirement. (Here I selected Yes.) and then press back or F3 and save the Investment Order.

Step 3: After saving the Investment Order, Profit Center gets updated in both IO and AUC. Below picture shows result of Profit center changed.

You might face some other issue depending on your system configuration.
Please feel free to ask addition questions if any or correct me if I am wrong.
Thank you!

SAP S/4HANA Finance

entry.title: Procedure For End-To-End Encryption And Signing Of File Through SFTP Via Integration Suite PART-1
entry.content:
I have a requirement where I must process a payment file generated in SAP through integration suite by encrypting and signing the file, sending the file to the bank server. We are sending and receiving the payment file through SFTP, to connect to the bank server, we have provided them with a PUBLIC SSH KEY. We were given the public key of the receiver, folder path to place the file, server address and credentials of the same to login and we must process the file generated in SAP to the receiver by encrypting and signing the file. We should encrypt the data using receiver’s public key and sign the same using our private key.

Procedure: -

I am providing a glimpse of usage of the SSH keys and public and private keys of both tenant and receiver below, SSH PRIVATE KEY of the tenant is used to connect the bank server, we share our SSH PUBLIC KEY to the receiver who is bank in our case so, they will map the SSH PUBLIC KEY in their system and provide us a user assigned to the SSH PUBLIC KEY. BANK PUBLIC KEY is provided to encrypt the payment file in CPI/INTEGRATION SUITE, TENANT PUBLIC KEY is given to the bank to verify the signature of the tenant where it is signed by the TENANT’s PRIVATE KEY in CPI/INTEGRATION SUITE. Lastly BANK’s PRIVATE KEY is used to decrypt the file when they receive the file from CPI through SFTP and they will verify the same using TENANT’s PUBLIC KEY. We must add the public key of the receiver and the public key and private key of ours generated through the procedure explained below to PGP keys in monitor -> manage security -> PGP Keys. Since this is a huge procedure, I will be sharing all the information related in two blog posts.

PROCEDURE TO GENERATE AND CONVERT PRIVATE PPK FORMAT KEY TO OPEN SSH KEY PEM FORMAT AND .P12 FORMAT THROUGH PUTTY KEY GENERATOR AND OPENSSL

How to generate a public and private SSH key using putty key generator (which will be used for authentication): -

Above picture shows the main screen of putty key generator

 

Steps to generate the SSH public and private keys: -

There is an option of generate and load at the bottom right of the screen shown above, click on generate and you will be getting below screen,

The above screen shows the generation of the public and private keys, just move the cursor as instructed above and the keys will be generated.

This is the screen showing the creation of the keys, just click on save public key and provide a name and location, the public key is saved, then click on the save private key by providing key passphrase and confirm the same and save, now you have successfully generated public and private keys using a putty key generator, public key is saved in file format and private key is saved in PPK format, but in order to save the keys in SAP CPI we need the file in .p12 format so we need to convert the PPK format file to .p12 format and for that we need OPENSSL.

 Converting the file from PPK to .p12:-

Here is the link to download OPENSSL for your system https://slproweb.com/products/Win32OpenSSL.html, using this download the OPENSSL and place private key which in PPK format in the bin folder of the OPENSSL, open command prompt in windows as an administrator and provide file path to the bin folder as a command, access dir of the bin folder and you should be able to see the private key file in PPK format you pasted in OPENSSL bin folder, https://www.kellton.com/kellton-tech-blog/how-to-generate-import-ssh-keys-in-sap-pi/po is the link with procedure explanation, using the following command openssl req -new -x509 -days 3650 -key <PrivateKey.pem> -out <x509_PrivateKey.pem> ,using the above command, you will be able to convert the file from PPK format to x509 PEM format, using the following command  openssl pkcs12 -export -in <x509_PrivateKey.pem> -inkey <PrivateKey.pem> -out <PrivateKey.p12>, using the above command, you will be able to convert the file from PEM x509 format to .p12 format which can be uploaded to SAP CPI keystore as a keypair.

Procedure for generating key pair (includes both public and private keys):

We need gpg4win to generate keys, we can find the required version to download and install by using the link here  https://files.gpg4win.org/gpg4win-2.3.4.exe, we require 2.3.4 version of gpg4win, after downloading the same please make sure to deselect the Kleopatra and select GPA while installing the components in the package, after installation procedure is completed, please create tenant specific directories and make sure to have three files specified gpa.conf, gpg.conf, run_gpa.bat in the same directory for key management, this procedure of creating tenant specific directories and placing the above files in the directory is mandatory for each tenant separately, find the link to download those file here Creating Tenant-Specific File Directories | SAP Help Portal, adapt the file run_gpa.bat by entering the path to the tenant-specific directory. These files are required to configure the usage of the GPA tool. The file run_gpa.bat sets the shell variable GNUPGHOME to the tenant-specific directory. The files gpa.conf and gpg.conf contain configurations for GPA and GPG. The file gpg.conf, for example, determines the strength of the applied encryption.

After running the GPA tool, you will be seeing a screen like this,

On the top you can find Windows, on clicking it you can find keyring manager, just click it and setup the keyring manager.

After setup you can find the screen like below,

Please go the keys in the top; after clicking the keys you can find an option of new key after clicking the new key you will be getting a popup like this,

Complete the procedure by filling up your name, mail id and passphrase and select the folder path where you want to save the keys, and you are now able to generate the keys. After the keys are generated, please find that the public and private keys both are saved in a single file, you can just copy and paste both in separate files through notepad/notepad++ and just upload the same in the PGP keys in manage security, monitor.

Procedure to consume the KEYS in to the PGP KEYS:

Please upload the keys generated through GPA to the PGP Keys in manage security below,

Go to PGP keys and there you can find an option of add like below,

Please go to add and there you can find public keys and secret keys options, upload public and secret keys separately and later you can observe both the keys will be in one type when you uploaded both public and secret keys, if not it shows only public or secret type in type, please find below reference image,

Till now, we have seen generating SSH keys and public private keys, we will see iflow design part in the next blog post.

 

Please let me know any points/suggestions regarding this blog post, so I will address the same in next blog post. We value your feedback.

entry.title: Power Up Your Networking and Knowledge in the Utilities and Energy Industry with SAP Community
entry.content:
Dear Utilities and Oil, Gas, and Energy Enthusiasts and Professionals. 

Are you looking to stay ahead of the latest trends and developments in the utilities and energy industry? Are you keen on networking with other professionals, gaining insights from industry experts, and expanding your knowledge base? If your answer is yes, then belonging to the SAP for Utilities and Oil, Gas, and Energy Community is a MUST for you!

There are several ways to engage with us:

The SAP Community is a thriving platform for professionals in the utilities and energy sector to come together, share ideas, ask questions, and stay updated on the latest innovations and best practices. Being active in the SAP Community will provide you access to a wealth of resources and opportunities to connect with like-minded individuals.

Join the SAP Utilities Group 

 

 

Join the SAP Oil, Gas, and Energy Group

 

 

By signing up for the newsletter, you will have access to valuable resources, including articles, blogs, webinars, whitepapers that cover a wide range of topics relevant to the utilities and energy industry. Whether you're interested in learning about the latest technologies, industry trends, or best practices, you'll find a wealth of information to fuel your professional growth.

Sign up for the Utilities Newsletter

 

 

Sign up for the Oil, Gas, and Energy Newsletter

 

 

Attending our events, particularly in-person, will greatly enhance your networking opportunities. In-person events offer a fantastic platform to connect with industry peers, thought leaders, and experts. By actively engaging in the community, you'll have the opportunity to participate in meaningful discussions, share ideas, contribute to the knowledge base, and establish valuable connections that can advance your career and business. 

Save the date for the SAP for Energy and Utilities Conference in Rotterdam from April 8-10, 2025 

Stay updated: In a fast-paced industry like utilities and energy, staying abreast of the latest developments is crucial. The SAP for Energy and Utilities Community channels help you stay updated on the latest news, product updates, and industry trends, ensuring that you're always ahead of the curve.

So, what are you waiting for? Join the SAP Community, sign up for the newsletter, and start enjoying the benefits of being an active participant in this vibrant community. Whether you're a customer, partner, or just interested in the utilities and energy sector for any reason, there's something for everyone in the SAP Community.

Watch the video 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

We can't wait to connect with you!

entry.title: Microsoft Office Integration + Scheduling Analysis for Office Reports in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Microsoft Office integration with SAP Analytics Cloud is a hot topic, especially now that it is possible to schedule and broadcast Analysis for Office reports in SAP Analytics Cloud.

SAP Product Manager Jeanne Bigonnet joined the APOS team recently in a webinar to provide an update on SAP Analytics Cloud Microsoft Office integration. The APOS team detailed new scheduling and broadcasting capabilities in their Publisher for Cloud solution.

You can watch this webinar on demand and download the slide deck here.

Agenda

Jeanne started by summarizing SAP’s MS integration offerings, including:

  • SAP Analytics Cloud, add-in for Microsoft Excel, which is delivered through the Microsoft Store and is built on an entirely different, Cloud-based code base from the older Analysis for Office offerings
  • SAP Analysis for Microsoft Office, also known as AfO, a mature product which continues to be an important add-in for data sources such as SAP BW, HANA, BPC, S/4 and Group Reporting

Jeanne told us that AfO has a user base of 1,000,000 users, which SAP will continue to support, but as per the most recent statement of direction, the add-in for Microsoft Excel constitutes the road ahead. As such, there is no roadmap for future AfO development, meaning that no new AfO capabilities will be developed by SAP. Jeanne clarified that this is why  the collaboration between SAP and APOS regarding AfO scheduling and broadcasting is so important.

AfO Statement of Direction

As mentioned, the add-in for Microsoft Excel is a Cloud product, which means it is fully integrated with SAC and Microsoft’s Office 365. Although the product was initially designed for Office 365, it is also compatible with Office 2021.

SAP Analytics Cloud Add-in for Microsoft Office XLS

Jeanne then went on to describe what new functionality has been delivered recently, including:

  • Support for Live SAP Business Warehouse (SAP BW/Tunnel) and SAP HANA Cloud in Microsoft Excel
  • Member Selector improvements: range
  • Conditional formatting
  • Repository / catalog integration

New connectivity for SAP BW and SAP HANA are great new additions.

As we will see later in the presentation, Repository/catalog integration is key for AfO scheduling and broadcasting.

AfO Recent Deliveries

Jeanne then told us about upcoming feature releases, including:

  • Advanced Flexible reporting – Asymmetric reporting: the new custom function SAP.ASYMMETRICFILTER lets customers build asymmetric reports; for example, a report showing Actual data for Q1 and only Forecast data for Q2 and Q3
  • Planning – Add Comments: customers can insert/retrieve data point comments entered from the web or from Excel.
  • Story Integration – Jump-off from Story: customers can easily jump off from the web to Excel Workbooks without any re-design action needed.

SAP Analytics Cloud Add-in for Microsoft XLS

Jeanne provided useful links for further exploration:

  • SAP Analytics Cloud add in for MS Excel – Community page & blogs & FAQ
  • SAP Analytics Cloud add in for MS Excel – Road Map
  • SAP Analytics Cloud add in for MS Excel –Deploying the add-in
  • SAP Analytics Cloud add in for MS Excel – See it in action!

Jeanne also provided useful links for the SAP Analytics Cloud, add-in for Microsoft Office PPT:

  • Video
  • Blog
  • SAP Help
  • Microsoft AppSource
  • Road Map Explorer

Scheduling AfO Reports in SAP Analytics Cloud

The conversation then turned to the topic of scheduling AfO reports in SAP Analytics Cloud. As you may know, SAP Analytics Cloud does not support AfO report scheduling, but APOS and SAP have collaborated to offer AfO report scheduling from SAP Analytics Cloud using the APOS Publisher for Cloud  solution. (Available on the SAP Store.)

Allan Pym of APOS asked Jeanne what she had heard from customers about the importance of AfO report scheduling and broadcasting. Jeanne answered that customers say they use scheduling to:

  • Schedule reports that normally take a long time to refresh. The benefit of scheduling is that the reports are ready for use when the user wants to work with them.
  • Automate their auditing. For example, they may schedule a report to refresh every day or every month and they can store them offline to record historical data.
  • Improve load balancing on their system. They schedule reports to refresh during the night when fewer users are on the system.

Allan then delivered an overview of Publisher for Cloud.

APOS Publisher for Cloud - AfO

Allan summarized APOS Publisher for Cloud’s capabilities and benefits:

  • High-volume publications of SAC Stories, SAC Applications, and Analysis for Office reports
    Reach a wide range of information consumers with PDF, XLS, CSV, PPT, DOC formats published to multiple destinations such as email, network file locations, and SFTP/FTP, and schedule pre-processing of Analysis for Office reports.
  • An alternative for SAP BusinessObjects scheduling & broadcasting use cases
    Remove barriers to SAP Analytics Cloud adoption, leverage SAC-based reports in the SAC Repository, and shift AfO scheduling away from BusinessObjects.
  • A path to modernization of BEx Broadcaster use cases
    Adopt Analysis for Office or SAP Analytics Cloud reports to fulfill current BEx reporting objectives, and remove S/4HANA and Datasphere deployment obstacles
  • Centralization and govern of Analysis for Office scheduling
    Monitor, manage and control Analysis for Office scheduling activities, understand and optimize AfO scheduling patterns, and reduce unnecessary scheduling and query activity.
  • The ability to push analytics to casual S/4HANA Users
    Remove information barriers related to casual S/4HANA users with infrequent needs for reports, improving SAP Analytis Cloud adoption, and supporting the broader user community information needs within S/4HANA modernization projects.

APOS Solution Consulting Manager Alan Golding then joined the discussion with a description of the solution’s architecture and use cases showing how it serves Analysis for Office report scheduling and broadcasting needs.

APOS Publisher for Cloud Architecture

Again, you can view the webinar and download the slide deck here, and I recommend doing so if you want to see Alan Golding’s demonstration of the personalization, scheduling, and broadcasting of Analysis for Office reports

APOS will take a deeper dive into this functionality in an upcoming webinar .

Q&A

The presentation ended with a lively Q&A on both the Microsoft Office integration update and the new Analysis for Office scheduling and broadcasting capabilities.

entry.title: A Brief History of SAP: An Introduction for the Uninitiated
entry.content:
SAP, short for Systems, Applications, and Products in Data Processing, is a global leader in enterprise resource planning (ERP) software. Founded in 1972, SAP has transformed how businesses manage their operations, finances, and resources.

Note:  This article is for someone unfamiliar with SAP. It explores the high-level history of SAP, covering its evolution, major milestones, and its impact on the business world over the last five decades. It's ideal for anyone looking to learn basic history and understand key developments.

The Early Years: 1972-1980

SAP was founded in Germany by five former IBM employees. The initial goal was to create a standard software solution to manage business processes. In 1973, they released their first product, SAP R/1, an integrated financial accounting system. This software was revolutionary for its time, allowing companies to process data in real-time and share information across departments.

Growth and Expansion: 1980s

As businesses recognized the need for integrated systems, SAP began to grow rapidly. In 1981, SAP introduced SAP R/2, which expanded its capabilities to support manufacturing, sales, and distribution processes. The software gained popularity among large enterprises in Germany and beyond.

By the mid-1980s, SAP was well-established in the German market and started expanding internationally. The company opened its first office outside Germany in the United States in 1988, marking the beginning of its global footprint.

The Birth of R/3: 1992

In 1992, SAP launched SAP R/3, a landmark product that transitioned the company from mainframe-based systems to a client-server architecture. R/3 provided a more flexible and user-friendly interface, allowing organizations to access and manage data across various functions, including finance, HR, and logistics.

R/3's success marked a significant turning point for SAP, as it enabled the company to capture a substantial share of the growing ERP market. By the late 1990s, SAP R/3 became synonymous with ERP solutions, helping businesses streamline their operations and improve efficiency.

SAP ECC 5.0 and ECC 6.0: 2000s

In the early 2000s, SAP introduced SAP ECC 5.0 (Enterprise Central Component) in 2004, which provided enhanced functionalities and improved integration capabilities over the previous R/3 version. ECC 5.0 emphasized process integration and included features such as enhanced business analytics and better user interfaces.

In 2006, SAP released SAP ECC 6.0, which became the foundation for many organizations looking to upgrade their ERP systems. ECC 6.0 offered significant improvements in performance, scalability, and user experience, integrating various modules for finance, supply chain, human resources, and more. This version marked a critical period in SAP's evolution, as it provided companies with tools to manage their entire operations efficiently.

Expanding Portfolio: SAP Business One, SuccessFactors, Fieldglass and many more

To cater to a broader market, SAP introduced several new products in the 2000s and 2010s:

  • SAP Business One: Launched in 2002, SAP Business One is designed for small to medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). It offers an integrated solution that helps manage business operations, including finance, sales, and customer relationships, allowing smaller organizations to benefit from ERP functionalities.

  • SuccessFactors: Acquired by SAP in 2012, SuccessFactors is a cloud-based human capital management (HCM) solution. It helps organizations manage various HR functions, including recruitment, performance management, and employee development, aligning workforce management with business strategy.

  • SAP Fieldglass: Also acquired in 2014, SAP Fieldglass specializes in managing external workforce and services procurement. It provides organizations with tools to manage contingent labor and services procurement, enhancing visibility and control over external resources.

SAP Modules: A Brief Overview

SAP offers a wide range of modules (65) that cater to various business functions. Some of the key modules include:

  1. SAP Financial Accounting (FI): Manages financial transactions and reporting.
  2. SAP Controlling (CO): Supports internal cost reporting and analysis.
  3. SAP Sales and Distribution (SD): Handles order processing, sales, and distribution logistics.
  4. SAP Material Management (MM): Manages procurement and inventory processes.
  5. SAP Production Planning (PP): Supports manufacturing and production processes.
  6. SAP Human Capital Management (HCM): Manages employee data, payroll, and recruitment.
  7. SAP Customer Relationship Management (CRM): Manages interactions with customers and prospects.
  8. SAP Supply Chain Management (SCM): Optimizes supply chain processes, including planning and logistics.
  9. SAP Business Intelligence (BI): Provides reporting and analytics capabilities for better decision-making.
  10. SAP Project System (PS): Manages project planning, execution, and control.

These modules can be integrated to provide a comprehensive solution tailored to an organization's specific needs.

New Millennium: SAP’s Innovations

Continuing its innovative streak, SAP introduced SAP ERP in 2004, further expanding its suite of applications. In 2006, SAP launched SAP Business Suite, which included various applications tailored for different industries, solidifying its position as a leader in the ERP market. This period also marked the beginning of SAP's focus on integrating analytics, business intelligence, and mobile capabilities into its offerings.

The Shift to Cloud: 2010s

As the business world shifted toward cloud computing, SAP recognized the need to adapt. In 2012, SAP launched SAP HANA, an in-memory database and computing platform that allowed businesses to analyze data in real-time, enabling quicker decision-making and improved operational efficiency.

SAP also embraced the cloud model, launching SAP S/4HANA in 2015. This next-generation ERP suite combined the capabilities of SAP HANA with a user-friendly interface and flexible deployment options, including on-premise and cloud solutions. S/4HANA represented a significant evolution in SAP's offerings, catering to modern business needs.

Recent Developments: 2020s and Beyond

In recent years, SAP has continued to innovate, focusing on sustainability, machine learning, and artificial intelligence. The company has emphasized helping businesses transition to more sustainable practices and integrate advanced technologies into their operations.

SAP's acquisition of companies like Qualtrics in 2019, a leader in experience management, reflects its commitment to delivering comprehensive solutions that address customer needs across various dimensions—product, customer, employee, and brand.

SAP Today: A Publicly Traded Leader

SAP is a publicly traded company, listed on the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) under the ticker symbol SAP. As one of the largest software companies in the world, SAP generates billions in annual revenue and serves thousands of clients across diverse industries. Its extensive portfolio includes not only ERP solutions but also products for customer experience, supply chain management, and human capital management.

SAP's solutions are utilized by many large organizations, including global giants like Coca-Cola, BMW, and Unilever, as well as privately held companies like Bosch, Cargill, and Koch Industries. The company also serves government clients, including the U.S. Department of Defense, NASA, and the United Nations. This diverse customer base demonstrates the reliability and scalability of SAP's offerings and underscores its reputation in various industries. The ability to serve both large enterprises and small-to-medium businesses (SMBs) through products like SAP Business One illustrates SAP's comprehensive market approach.

SAP has strategically expanded its operations worldwide, establishing key offices in regions such as North America, Europe, and Asia-Pacific. This global footprint allows SAP to cater to local markets while leveraging international resources and expertise. The company emphasizes partnerships with local firms to enhance its service delivery and customer engagement in these regions.

In terms of market position, SAP competes with other major players such as Oracle, Microsoft, and Workday. While Oracle is a strong competitor in the ERP space, particularly for large enterprises, SAP maintains a significant market share, especially in industries such as manufacturing and logistics. Microsoft, through its Dynamics 365 platform, offers integrated solutions that appeal to a wide range of businesses, while Workday focuses primarily on HCM and financial management solutions.

SAP's ongoing commitment to innovation and customer success positions it as a leader in the ERP and business software markets, making it a key player in shaping the future of enterprise technology.

Conclusion

Today, SAP is a dominant player in the ERP software market, serving thousands of organizations worldwide across various industries. With a rich history spanning over five decades, SAP has continually evolved to meet the changing demands of businesses. Its journey from a small German startup to a global leader exemplifies the importance of innovation, adaptability, and a commitment to customer success.

For those unfamiliar with SAP, understanding its history provides valuable context for its role in shaping the modern business landscape. As organizations continue to seek integrated solutions for managing their operations, SAP's legacy and future innovations will undoubtedly play a significant role in their success.

I wrote this article because family and friends often ask me what SAP is when I mention I've worked with this ERP throughout my career. I aimed to keep it simple and easy to understand, even for those unfamiliar with the topic. If any enhancements or corrections are needed, feel free to message me privately, and I'll be happy to review and update it as required.

entry.title: What we think about the second meetup in New York
entry.content:
A Look Back at Our Second NYC #SAP Meetup

We just had our second #SAP meetup in New York City, and it was fantastic! It was a smaller group this time, but everyone was really into SAP and excited to be there.

We planned to start the event two hours before sunset, which allowed us to enjoy the park's light and gave participants enough time to arrive. The park was an excellent choice for our meetup. Its quiet and comfortable atmosphere was perfect for us to get to know each other better and have meaningful conversations.

 

Meet Some of Our Attendees

One of the attendees, Richard, was especially excited. He’s currently an accountant in the fashion industry but is passionate about technology. He’s been learning about SAP FICO on his own after hearing about SAP's strength in big companies. He came to the meetup eager to meet others and grow his network.

Another attendee has recently moved to the U.S. and is a director at his company. Like many of us, he wasn’t born in the U.S., which added to our diverse mix. We ended up sharing stories about visas and the immigration process, which got quite a few laughs!

Despite our initial timing, we decided to stay longer because we had fluid conversations and a lot to talk about. We were a diverse group of developers, functional experts, data scientists, and Basis team members.

What’s Next?

We’re already planning a big event, probably a SIT, for this winter or next year and a relaxed get-together next month. Stay tuned for more details, and we hope to see you there! 
The goal is to find new attendees who are ready to share and be an event speakers.

In the end, thanks to everyone who came. The park's great location, your energy, and stories made this meetup special. Let's keep the spirit alive as we continue to learn and connect through SAP!

entry.title: How it was SAP Inside Track Porto 2024
entry.content:
Saturday, 28 SEP we had the first SAP Inside Track event in Porto.
In fact, it started on the 27th SET with an SAP CodeJam taught by Vitaly, which included a day of training, interspersed with coffee breaks in the morning and afternoon.
The event was organized by the following:
-Manuel Robalinho
-Arnaldo Calçada
-Pedro Rodrigues
-Pedro Marques
-Pedro Vieira
-Fatima Linhares
-Matheus Brazil
-Antonio Elias
 
As volunteers, we had the following colleagues:
-António Linhares Oliveira
-Simone Thami de Queiroz
-João Calçada
-Heyd Magnus
-Ernesto Martelo Junior
-Eloisa Silva
-Micael Mesquita
-Yvana Boufleuer
-Catarina Robalinho
-Carolina Albuquerque
-Luciana Caliman
 
The event was led by Arnaldo Calçada and Fátima Linhares. It was planned for 200 participants, and in terms of registrations, it was complete. 
It took place at The Lodge Hotel in Porto (located in Ribeira de Gaia), which proved very good, beautiful, and versatile for this type of event.
 
The event had coffee breaks in the morning and afternoon, and the event hotel offered lunch for organizers, volunteers, and sponsors.
 
At the reception for participants, there was a bag and an event T-shirt for everyone.
 
We had photographic coverage by a Volunteer (Kelzia Robalinho) and short interviews with participants to hear about the organization, lectures, and location. This allowed us to gather lessons learned and suggestions from various sources.
 
The topics and speakers of the lectures were:
0. SAP Communities and Knowledge Sharing on SAP Inside Track by Marco Santos
1. SAP Project: Technology or business? by Antonio Andrade
2. The era of Digitalization and Sustainability by Filipe Silva
3. Global eCompliance with SAP Document and Reporting Compliance by Felipe Borges
4. SAP Implementation Change Management how to manage organizations and people by Marta Mota
5. SAP Integration Suite - Security Guidelines - what can go wrong? by Antonio Vaz
6. SAP HANA multi-model examples by Rudnicki, Witalij
7. People, Process and Technology as successful enablers for excellence in industrial operations by Tiago Fernandes
8. Gen AI by Bruno Costa
9. SAP Build Work Zone Content Federation deep dive by Fabio Pagoti
10. AI capabilities and data security issues by Abílio Cardoso
 
At the end,Tuna Academica do ISEP we had a musical attraction by Tuna Académica from ISEP, who rocked with their brilliance!
 
The entire event was broadcast via YouTube, on the page https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=miFkEAnpLuk
 
Link to event fotos: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1gFas-vC-gQjsAkaSExQZyXrPNQC5b0o2?usp=sharing
 
Link to the PDF lectures: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1pCkMvXah4T-vpK1awKvzV2MILstYql_y?usp=drive_link
